Skip to main content

RealDoor

Ready, not eligible.

We get your file to the person who decides, complete the first time you hand it over. What we cannot tell you is the outcome — and no software can, because that decision needs checks that are not in these documents. Nothing on this screen means approved, denied, or ineligible; a qualified housing professional decides that.

Get your housing documents ready for a person to review

Hand your file over once, with nothing missing. RealDoor reads the documents for one household, shows you where on the page every value came from, and tells you what is still missing or out of date — so the person who reads it is not sending it back to you for a paper you could have brought the first time. What we cannot tell you is the outcome, and no software can: that decision needs income, household and status checks that are not in these documents. A housing professional makes it.

Data source: loading…

Before you start

This walkthrough takes about ten minutes. The documents for the household you choose above are already loaded, so you can go straight through it without uploading anything. Step 1 also lets you read a synthetic document of your own, held in memory for this session only. Nothing is sent anywhere. You can stop at any step and your work stays on this device.

What happens, in order

    Step 1 of 6

    Check the values we read from your documents

    Each value below is shown together with the box on the page it was read from. Choose a field name to light up its box. Nothing here is inferred about the person.

    Step 2 of 6

    Correct a value we read wrong

    Change a value the system read, and watch the numbers underneath it move — or watch the system explain why your correction was not used.

    Step 3 of 6

    Ask what a housing rule says

    Every answer carries its rule id, the authority behind it, the date it took effect, and where in the source it is written.

    Step 4 of 6

    See how your yearly income figure was worked out

    Inputs, formula, result, threshold, comparison, effective date. A comparison is not a determination.

    Step 5 of 6

    See what is missing or out of date

    What is present, what is missing, what has expired, and what could not be dated — with the one thing you can do about each.

    Step 6 of 6

    Check what we found, then take your packet

    This is everything the earlier steps produced, in one place. Change anything that is wrong before you download it.

    Not part of the renter's six steps

    How this works, and how we tested it

    This page is for anyone checking the service rather than using it. Every control below can be triggered here and its actual response shown — a disclaimer is not a control. Underneath are our own measurements, printed as they came out.

    Where the challenge's six acceptance steps live

    The challenge brief specifies a six-step acceptance demo. Our numbered walkthrough is written for the renter, not for that list, so the two do not line up one to one. This is the mapping.

    • 1. Upload documents, show extraction evidence — walkthrough step 1. The household's documents are pre-loaded, and the panel at the top of that step reads a synthetic document you upload yourself. Either way every value carries the box on the page it came from. An uploaded document is read on its own and is not added to the household, because working out whose document it is and what it replaces would mean guessing.
    • 2. Edit one field, show downstream values update — walkthrough step 2.
    • 3. Ask a rule question, show an authoritative citation — walkthrough step 3.
    • 4. Deterministic calculation with effective dates — walkthrough step 4.
    • 5. Identify missing or expired items, export a packet — walkthrough steps 5 and 6. We split it because reviewing what is open and taking the packet are two different decisions for the renter.
    • 6. Run the refusal, prompt-injection and session-deletion tests — this page, immediately below. It is not numbered in the walkthrough because it is not a task the renter performs. Both the adversarial suite and the static no-decision guard exercise these paths on every run.

    Why the screens are worded the way they are

    The open-questions rail is always visible and never folded away. A system that knows something and does not say it is the failure this product exists to prevent, so the things we are unsure about get a permanent rail rather than a disclosure the renter has to think to open.

    An empty list means nothing was withheld, not that nothing was checked. When a household's abstentions list comes back empty, that is a measured result — every value needed was read from a document and every required item is accounted for — and not the absence of a measurement.

    We say what we cannot do, and why we cannot do it. The service reports readiness and never eligibility. That is not a decision we are holding back: an eligibility determination needs third-party income verification, household-composition proof and status checks that do not exist in a document set, so no software can produce one from this input. What we can do is make the handoff to the person who does decide complete the first time. Getting a file there sooner is not the same as getting a better answer out of it, and we do not claim the second.

    The data behind this demo

    The households in the picker are synthetic. The renter's screens call this an example household with sample documents, which is what it is; the technical version is that this build ships ui/dist/fixtures.js, captured from a real run of the same pipeline that serves the live API, so the offline page shows genuine extraction output with no server and no network behind it.

    The data source in use is named under the household picker on every screen. If a server is running and you want the offline fixtures instead, add ?fixtures to the URL.

    The controls, demonstrated live

    Our own numbers

    Our measured results, including the parts we did not run. Published as measured, whether or not they flatter us.

    What this system is unsure about

    Anything we could not read, or could not be sure of, is listed here — on every screen, whichever step you are on.

    About this build

    No external fonts, scripts, images, or analytics. Every request this page makes goes to the address in the bar above it and nowhere else; opened as a file, it makes none at all. That is a promise about this page, not about the whole system: when the question classifier is switched on, the server sends your question sentence to a model provider so it can pick a topic label. What we can promise structurally is narrower and harder: no document, no extracted field and no household record is ever part of that request. Before your sentence is sent we replace the identifier shapes we can recognise, such as an email address, a phone number or a street address. We cannot recognise all of them, so a name you type is sent as you typed it.
